This height difference and volume distribution maximize natural circulation driving head.

The RCS volumes in Table 5.1-1 (e.g., hot leg: 635 ft³, cold leg: 578 ft³, SG region: 621 ft³, PZR region: 578 ft³) are distributed along the vertical flow path shown in Figure 5.1-2, ensuring large, unobstructed regions for upward (hot leg/SG) and downward (cold leg) flow, which supports stable natural circulation by minimizing flow resistance and maintaining effective thermal stratification and driving head.  
Source: Table 5.1-1; Figure 5.1-2; [nuscale_ch05_0004], [nuscale_ch05_0006].

results.question: Compare the 100% and minimum flow conditions from Table 5.1-2 and explain the impact on natural circulation as described in the text.
results.expected_answer: Table 5.1-2 shows at 100% power flow is 587 kg/s with delta-T of 99.8 degrees F, while minimum design flow is 538.5 kg/s (91.7%) with delta-T of 107.6 degrees F. The text explains that lower flow increases core temperature rise, which actually increases the buoyancy driving force for natural circulation, providing a self-regulating characteristic.
results.agent_answer:
At 100% flow, the core flow rate is 587.0 kg/s with a core ΔT of 99.8°F, while at minimum flow (91.7% of nominal), the flow rate drops to 538.5 kg/s and core ΔT increases to 107.6°F; the text explains that natural circulation is maintained under both conditions, but reduced flow increases core temperature rise, which is accounted for in the design to ensure safe operation.  
(Source: Table 5.1-2; nuscale_ch05_0006 System Evaluation)

results.question: Compare the LTOP setpoints from Table 5.2-10 with the P-T limit curves described in Tables 5.3-6/7, explaining their relationship.
results.expected_answer: Table 5.2-10 LTOP setpoints (380/615.5/850 psia at 193/240/277 degrees F) provide overpressure protection below the P-T limit curves in Tables 5.3-6/7. The LTOP setpoints are set well below the P-T limits at each temperature to ensure that even with LTOP valve response time and overshoot, the P-T limits are not exceeded.
results.agent_answer:
The LTOP setpoints in Table 5.2-10 (e.g., 380 psig at ≤193°F, 615.5 psig at 240°F, 850 psig at ≥277°F) are selected to ensure that reactor coolant system pressure does not exceed the P-T limit curves in Tables 5.3-6/7, meaning the LTOP setpoints always remain below the allowable pressures defined by the P-T curves for each temperature, thus protecting vessel integrity during low-temperature operations (see also Figures 5.2-4, 5.3-3, and 5.3-5).  
Sources: Table 5.2-10, Tables 5.3-6/7, Figures 5.2-4, 5.3-3, 5.3-5.

results.question: Using Table 5.3-3 and Figure 5.3-1, compare the irradiation effects on different RPV regions and explain why the weld has the highest ART.
results.expected_answer: Table 5.3-3 shows ARTs: shell 92.2 degrees F, weld 120.4 degrees F, bottom head 77.4 degrees F. Figure 5.3-1 shows the RPV cross section where the weld in the beltline region is directly adjacent to the core at the elevation of peak neutron fluence. The weld's higher initial RTNDT plus higher fluence exposure produces the highest ART.
results.agent_answer: At 57 EFPY, the weld region of the RPV has the highest Adjusted Reference Temperature (ART) at 120.4°F, compared to 92.2°F for the shell and 77.4°F for the bottom head; this is because the weld contains higher initial RTNDT and higher copper and nickel content, making it more susceptible to irradiation embrittlement (Table 5.3-3, Figure 5.3-1).

results.question: Compare the NuScale gross and net electrical output (Ch.01) with the reactor pool temperature constraint (Ch.05).
results.expected_answer: Each module produces 50 MWe gross, with 12 modules yielding 570 MWe net (Ch.01). Ch.05 specifies a 140 degrees F reactor pool temperature limit. The pool must absorb rejected heat from all modules during DHRS operation while staying below this limit.

The NuScale Power Module has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a net output of 570 MWe for a 12-module plant (Table 1.2-1), and this output is constrained by the reactor pool temperature, which must be reduced below 420°F within 36 hours after shutdown as shown in the decay heat removal system performance (nuscale_ch05_0022, Figures 5.4-13, 5.4-15).

results.question: Compare the safety enhancement from integral RCS design (Ch.01) with the specific LOCA categories eliminated per Ch.05.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 states the integral RCS within the RPV eliminates large-bore piping and LBLOCA. Ch.05 confirms that without external RCS loops, the largest possible break is limited to small instrument/CVCS lines, making all LOCAs small-break events manageable by ECCS and DHRS.
results.agent_answer:
The integral RCS design enhances safety by eliminating external loop piping, pump seals, and large external penetrations, which in turn eliminates LOCA categories such as large-break LOCA, pump seal LOCA, and piping break LOCA within the reactor coolant pressure boundary (as specified in Ch.05) from the safety analysis scope (Ch.01, Ch.05).  
Sources: [nuscale_ch05_0019], [nuscale_ch01_0779], [nuscale_ch01_0731], [nuscale_ch05_0012].

results.question: Compare the NuScale passive safety approach described in Ch.01 with the specific passive systems detailed in Ch.05.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes NuScale's philosophy of passive safety using natural forces. Ch.05 details the specific implementations: natural circulation for core cooling (no RCPs), DHRS with passive condensers for decay heat removal, ECCS with gravity-driven recirculation valves, and vacuum CNV for containment pressure suppression.
results.agent_answer:
Chapter 1 describes NuScale’s passive safety approach as relying on natural circulation, a vacuum containment vessel, and passive systems like the ECCS for decay heat removal without external power, while Chapter 5 details these systems specifically as the reactor coolant system (RCS) using natural circulation (no pumps), the ECCS with vent and recirculation valves, and containment-integrated heat removal—all implemented as shown in Figures 5.1-1 to 5.1-3 and Table 1.2-2.  
Sources: [nuscale_ch01_0021], [nuscale_ch01_0024], [nuscale_ch05_0003], Table 1.2-2, Figures 5.1-1, 5.1-2, 5.1-3, 1.2-9.

results.question: Compare the description of the NuScale SG in Ch.01's general design with the detailed performance specifications in Ch.05.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes the SG as helical coil type integrated within the RPV using a Rankine cycle. Ch.05 provides detailed specifications: design pressure 2100 psia, feedwater inlet ~300 degrees F, steam outlet ~575 degrees F, flow rate ~71.4 lbm/sec per SG, with primary flow on the shell side.
results.agent_answer:
Chapter 01 describes the NuScale SG as a vertical helical coil, once-through design integrated within the reactor pressure vessel with approximately 18,000 ft² heat transfer area and 1,380 tubes per module, while Chapter 05 provides detailed specifications: 159.13 MWt heat transfer, 500.1 psia outlet pressure, 584.4°F outlet temperature, 0.625-inch tube OD, 0.050-inch wall thickness, and 21 tube columns per module (see Table 1.3-1, Table 5.4-1, Table 5.4-2).  
Sources: nuscale_ch01_0052, nuscale_ch05_0019, Table 1.3-1, Table 5.4-1, Table 5.4-2.

results.question: How does the 12-module plant concept in Ch.01 relate to the single-module DHRS analysis in Ch.05?
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes a 12-module plant with shared reactor pool. Ch.05 analyzes DHRS for a single module but notes the reactor pool must accommodate heat rejection from multiple modules simultaneously. The pool temperature limit of 140 degrees F applies to the shared pool with all modules potentially requiring DHRS.
results.agent_answer:
The 12-module plant concept in Ch.01 establishes the overall facility context, while the single-module DHRS analysis in Ch.05 provides detailed safety and performance evaluation for one module, which is representative and applicable to each of the up to 12 independent modules in the plant; thus, the plant-level design frames and bounds the scope of the module-level DHRS analysis.  
(Source: nuscale_ch01_0001, nuscale_ch01_0003, nuscale_ch05_0022, nuscale_ch05_0025)

results.question: Compare the Ch.01 description of NuScale's eliminated RCPs with the Ch.05 analysis of natural circulation flow rates.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 lists RCPs as eliminated, with 0 pumps compared to 4 in a typical PWR. Ch.05 quantifies the natural circulation flow: 587 kg/s at 100% power, adequate for heat removal at NuScale's power density. The absence of RCPs eliminates loss-of-flow accidents and RCP seal LOCAs.
results.agent_answer: Chapter 01 states that NuScale eliminates reactor coolant pumps (RCPs) and relies solely on passive natural circulation, making pump-related accident scenarios inapplicable, while Chapter 05 confirms that the system achieves core flow rates of 587 kg/s (best estimate at 100% power) via natural circulation, ensuring adequate cooling without forced flow (see Table 5.1-2 and [nuscale_ch01_0730], [nuscale_ch05_0003]).

results.question: Compare the NuScale design life from Ch.01 with the RPV embrittlement projections in Ch.05.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 specifies a 60-year design life. Ch.05 projects RPV embrittlement to 57 EFPY with adjusted reference temperatures (max 120.4 degrees F for welds) well below PTS screening criteria of 270/300 degrees F, confirming adequate margin for the 60-year life.
results.agent_answer:
The NuScale design life is 60 years (Table 1.3-1), and RPV embrittlement projections in Ch.05 (Tables 5.3-3, 5.3-8, 5.3-9) show that reference temperature shifts, Charpy upper-shelf energy, and pressurized thermal shock screening values remain within regulatory limits for up to 57 effective full-power years, supporting safe operation for the full design life.  
Sources: nuscale_ch01_0052, Table 1.3-1; nuscale_ch05_0016, Tables 5.3-3, 5.3-8, 5.3-9.

results.question: Compare the Ch.01 overview of containment features with the Ch.05 detailed containment role in ECCS operation.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes the CNV as a steel vessel under vacuum providing passive containment. Ch.05 explains the CNV's dual role in ECCS: it receives steam from RVVs which condenses on the cool CNV wall, and RRVs return condensed water to the RPV. The CNV effectively acts as both containment barrier and ECCS component.
results.agent_answer: The Ch.01 overview describes the containment as a passive steel vessel that condenses steam and collects water during ECCS operation, while Ch.05 details that during ECCS actuation, containment serves as the heat sink for decay heat removal and as the collection point for recirculated coolant, with no containment spray or sump systems present in NuScale’s design (see Figure 1.2-9 and Table 1.3-1) [nuscale_ch01_0024, nuscale_ch01_0167, nuscale_ch01_0187, nuscale_ch01_0189].

results.question: Compare the NuScale general description of the CVCS from Ch.01 with the detailed chemistry specifications in Ch.05.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes the CVCS as providing boron control and water chemistry management.
